CurrentUseIn13: Model parameter: match_elasticity
RecommendedUse: Use as baseline and sensitivity range for match_elasticity.
Source: Lake, Shamiri, Sharma and Bialowas 2024, ABS/RBA conference paper
Notes: Paper reports unemployed elasticity about 0.326; current model uses 0.42 as a CRS-normalized baseline from previous 12b convention.

CurrentUseIn13: Implicit counterpart to match_elasticity under CRS normalization
RecommendedUse: Use as cross-check because Australian estimates imply vacancies matter more than unemployed stocks.
Source: Lake, Shamiri, Sharma and Bialowas 2024, ABS/RBA conference paper
Notes: Paper reports vacancy elasticity 0.450; useful because the sum of reported elasticities is below one.

CurrentUseIn13: Benchmark for matching efficiency, not a direct level target
RecommendedUse: Use only to benchmark changes in mu; model mu is normalized.
Source: Lake, Shamiri, Sharma and Bialowas 2024, ABS/RBA conference paper
Notes: Reported efficiency is 0.841 over the study period; not directly comparable to weekly model mu.

CurrentUseIn13: Motivates using mu_post to fit NZ post JFR
RecommendedUse: Estimate from ABS flows and vacancies for the exact population/time window.
Availability: Australian data framework available; sample-specific moment should be estimated
Source: Australian Treasury Round Up 2024, matching across skills and regions
Notes: Treasury defines matching probability as hires from job searchers divided by previous-period job searchers and tightness as vacancies/searchers.

Moment: Monthly labour-force gross flows during COVID
CurrentUseIn13: Not directly used; useful validation for monthly transitions
RecommendedUse: Use to validate model-implied JFR and separation flows in COVID months.
Availability: Australian data article; exact sample-specific monthly flows should be estimated
Source: ABS, Flows into and out of employment and unemployment
Notes: ABS article reports May-June 2020 unemployed-to-employed flow of 21.5% and April-May 2020 of 19.3%; adapt carefully to sample frequency.

CurrentUseIn13: Not directly used; informs separation/job-to-job sensitivity
RecommendedUse: Use as an upper-level benchmark for voluntary/job-to-job mobility.
Source: ABS, Job mobility February 2024
Notes: ABS reports 8% job mobility in 2024 and 9.6% in 2023; useful for voluntary/job-to-job margin, not direct job-loss separations.

Moment: Annual left/lost job and retrenchment rates
CurrentUseIn13: Not directly used; informs separation decomposition
RecommendedUse: Use to distinguish quits/job-to-job changes from involuntary loss.
Source: ABS, Job mobility February 2024
Notes: ABS reports 2.1 million left/lost a job and a 1.7% annual retrenchment rate in year ending Feb 2024.

CurrentUseIn13: Not directly used; informs plausible separation range
RecommendedUse: Use as broad external check; not job-loser-specific weekly hazard.
Source: Sila 2019 OECD/HILDA job displacement paper
Notes: OECD/HILDA reports all moves out of jobs around 20% annually in Australia circa 2015/2016.

CurrentUseIn13: Implemented structurally through accept_prob; not yet calibrated to this moment
RecommendedUse: Calibrate offer_threshold/offer_dispersion once a comparable acceptance/reservation moment is chosen.
Availability: Australian literature, older and not COVID-specific
Source: RBA RDP 1999-02, Reservation Wages and Duration of Unemployment
Notes: RBA finds reservation wages had little effect on duration; few offers appear more binding, so acceptance margin should be disciplined cautiously.

CurrentUseIn13: Not directly used; informs sample selection and NILF transitions
RecommendedUse: Use to decide whether NILF transitions or marginal attachment should be added.
Availability: Australian official statistic; not necessarily same sample
Source: ABS, Potential workers February 2024
Notes: ABS reports 1.9 million potential workers in Feb 2024; use to think about participation/NILF extension.

CurrentUseIn13: Used to set benefit_multiplier and supplement shock
RecommendedUse: Use as known policy shock; do not estimate this from the DiD.
Source: Parliamentary Library/PBO summaries of JobSeeker COVID measures
Notes: The supplement almost doubled the maximum JobSeeker payment, so the rate change should remain treated as known.

CurrentUseIn13: Not directly used; candidate shifter for search-cost/mutual-obligation channel
RecommendedUse: Use as institutional timing for search-cost or compliance shock, not as benefit generosity.
Source: Parliamentary Library, Changes to COVID-19 social security measures
Notes: Mutual obligation suspension/reintroduction is a separate policy shock that may alter search costs independently of payment generosity.

Moment: Endogenous search effort maps concavely into offers
CurrentUseIn13: Implemented through p_offer = 1 - exp(-contact * search)
RecommendedUse: Use as the core endogenous-search probability mapping; search_curvature is calibrated across the health-shock grid.
Source: Shimer 2004, Search Intensity
Notes: Shimer uses p = 1 - (1 - mu(theta))^s for multiple applications; this script uses the continuous approximation p = 1 - exp(-contact * s).

CurrentUseIn13: Not directly used; candidate prior for kappa/search elasticity
RecommendedUse: International fallback only; replace with Australian admin/search-report moment if available.
Source: Krueger and Mueller 2010, US time-use evidence
Notes: Fallback only; use Australian administrative search/reporting data if available.

CurrentUseIn13: Varied as main sensitivity parameter
RecommendedUse: Currently missing; estimate or bound using health, lockdown, local outbreak, household, financial-stress, and welfare-exclusion variables. Do not use occupation/industry here because the study estimates already control for them.
Availability: Missing; estimate from administrative and exposure data
Source: No clean Australian moment found
Notes: This is the central missing moment for whether DiD overstates or understates the benefit-only effect.
